name: Museum of the Bible
description: A nondenominational museum near the National Mall dedicated to the history, narrative, and cultural impact of the Bible. Its permanent collection includes rare early Bibles and manuscripts, Dead Sea Scroll fragments, and galleries tracing the Bible's role in American history and around the world. Founded in 2017 by the evangelical Green family, owners of Hobby Lobby.

name: Billy Graham Center Museum, Wheaton College
description: A museum on the campus of Wheaton College tracing the life and worldwide preaching ministry of evangelist Billy Graham, from his rural North Carolina upbringing through decades of evangelistic crusades broadcast around the world. Housed in Billy Graham Hall alongside the Billy Graham Center's archives of evangelical history.

name: Bob Jones University Museum & Gallery
address: 1700 Wade Hampton Blvd, Greenville, SC 29614, USA
description: One of the largest collections of religious art in the United States, assembled by Bob Jones Jr. beginning in 1948 and featuring Old Master paintings on biblical and Christian themes by artists including Rubens, Botticelli, and van Dyck, alongside a smaller collection of ancient Near Eastern and biblical-era artifacts. The university itself drew scrutiny for a ban on interracial dating that remained in place until 2000.
status: Closed to walk-in visitors as of 2026 while relocating to a new site in downtown Greenville; group tours available by reservation

name: Creation Museum
address: 2800 Bullittsburg Church Rd, Petersburg, KY 41080, USA
description: A museum operated by the young-earth creationist ministry Answers in Genesis, presenting the biblical account of creation, the Genesis flood, and the Fall as a framework for interpreting natural history, alongside a botanical garden and planetarium. Its young-earth reading of Genesis is a specific theological position not shared by all evangelicals, many of whom hold old-earth or non-literal views of the creation days.

name: Ark Encounter
address: 1 Ark Encounter Dr, Williamstown, KY 41097, USA
description: A themed attraction near Cincinnati built around a full-scale wooden replica of Noah's Ark, operated by the same young-earth creationist ministry as the Creation Museum, Answers in Genesis. Exhibits inside the Ark present the Genesis flood narrative; as with the Creation Museum, its young-earth position is one specific view within a wider range of evangelical positions on Genesis.

name: ICR Discovery Center for Science & Earth History
description: A museum in Dallas operated by the Institute for Creation Research presenting young-earth creationist interpretations of geology, biology, and earth history through interactive exhibits and a planetarium. Like the Creation Museum and Ark Encounter, its young-earth position is a specific theological stance not held by all evangelicals.

name: Mennonite Heritage Center
description: A museum, library, and genealogical archive in Harleysville, Pennsylvania preserving the history of Mennonite and other Anabaptist communities in southeastern Pennsylvania, including the Mary Jane Lederach Hershey Fraktur Gallery of Pennsylvania German folk art and the John L. Ruth Historical Library.

name: Library of Congress — Gutenberg Bible
address: Thomas Jefferson Building, 101 Independence Ave SE, Washington, DC 20540, USA
description: One of only three complete vellum copies of the Gutenberg Bible in the United States is on permanent public display in the Library of Congress's Thomas Jefferson Building — the first substantial book printed from movable type in the West, circa 1455.
interest_note: A secular national library, not a Christian institution, but its free, permanent Gutenberg Bible display is a landmark for anyone interested in the history of how the Bible came to be printed and widely distributed.
status: Open to the public, free admission

name: Morgan Library & Museum
address: 225 Madison Ave, New York, NY 10016, USA
description: A research library and museum in New York founded on financier J. Pierpont Morgan's personal collection. It holds three copies of the Gutenberg Bible — more than any other institution in the world — plus one of the finest collections of medieval and Renaissance illuminated manuscripts in the United States, including numerous Books of Hours and biblical texts.
interest_note: A secular institution, but its manuscript holdings are an unmatched resource for the history of the Bible's transmission and medieval Christian devotional life.

name: Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale University
address: 121 Wall St, New Haven, CT 06511, USA
description: Yale University's rare book library, whose iconic marble-paneled building houses a Gutenberg Bible on permanent public display on its mezzanine, alongside one of the world's earliest known printed texts.
interest_note: A secular university library, freely open to visitors, with a permanently displayed Gutenberg Bible of interest to anyone studying the history of the printed Bible.
status: Open to the public, free admission

name: New York Public Library — Polonsky Exhibition (Gutenberg Bible)
address: Stephen A. Schwarzman Building, 476 Fifth Ave, New York, NY 10018, USA
description: The New York Public Library's flagship Stephen A. Schwarzman Building hosts the Polonsky Exhibition of the Library's Treasures, a free permanent exhibition that includes a Gutenberg Bible acquired in 1847 by library founder James Lenox, alongside other historic manuscripts and printed texts.
interest_note: A secular public library, but the permanently displayed Gutenberg Bible is of interest to anyone tracing the printed Bible's early history in America.
status: Open to the public, free admission

name: Huntington Library
address: 1151 Oxford Rd, San Marino, CA 91108, USA
description: A research library, art museum, and botanical garden in San Marino, California, whose permanent exhibition includes one of only three vellum Gutenberg Bibles in the United States, alongside other rare early printed and manuscript Bibles.
interest_note: A secular institution, but its Gutenberg Bible and early Bible collection are of significant interest for the history of the printed Scriptures.

name: National Museum of African American History and Culture
address: 1400 Constitution Ave NW, Washington, DC 20560, USA
description: The Smithsonian's museum on the National Mall dedicated to African American history and culture includes a permanent exhibition on the central role of Christian faith in Black American life — from the religion of the enslaved through the Black church's role in the civil rights movement — alongside artifacts such as a Bible owned by Nat Turner and items belonging to Richard Allen, founder of the African Methodist Episcopal Church.
interest_note: A secular, government-run museum, but its permanent religion exhibition is a significant resource on the history and faith of the Black church in America.
status: Open to the public, free admission (timed passes required)

name: National Civil Rights Museum
description: Built around the former Lorraine Motel in Memphis, where Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated on April 4, 1968, this museum traces the history of the American civil rights movement, including the room where King stayed on the night of his death.
interest_note: A secular museum of civil rights history, but a significant site for the intersection of Christian faith and public witness, given Dr. King's ministry and the movement's roots in the Black church.

name: British Library — Sir John Ritblat Treasures Gallery
description: The British Library's free permanent gallery of its greatest treasures, including the Lindisfarne Gospels (c. 700), a leaf of the Codex Sinaiticus — one of the two earliest surviving Christian Bibles — an original Tyndale New Testament, a Gutenberg Bible, and Handel's manuscript score for Messiah.
interest_note: A secular national library, but this single free gallery holds an unmatched concentration of landmark items in the history of the Bible's text and transmission.
status: Open to the public, free admission

name: Bodleian Libraries — Weston Library Treasury, Oxford
description: The Bodleian Library's free permanent exhibition space in the Weston Library, showcasing rotating highlights from Oxford University's collections, among them one of the library's copies of the Gutenberg Bible.
interest_note: A secular university library; its Bible-history holdings are of interest, though the Treasury's displays rotate and a Gutenberg Bible is not guaranteed to be on view at every visit.
status: Open to the public, free admission; displays rotate

name: Huguenot Museum, Rochester
address: 95 High St, Rochester, Kent ME1 1LX, England
description: Britain's only museum dedicated to the Huguenots — French Reformed Protestants who fled Catholic persecution in France after the 1685 revocation of the Edict of Nantes. Housed by the French Hospital, a charity founded in 1718 to shelter poor Huguenot refugees, the museum traces their flight, settlement in England, and lasting contribution to English trades and church life.

name: Hereford Cathedral — Mappa Mundi and Chained Library
address: 5 College Cloisters, Cathedral Close, Hereford HR1 2NG, England
description: The largest surviving chained library in the world, housed at this Church of England cathedral, with some 1,500 books — including early Bibles and biblical commentaries — still attached to their medieval reading desks by chain, alongside the 13th-century Mappa Mundi.

name: International Museum of the Reformation, Geneva
description: A museum beside Geneva's Cathédrale Saint-Pierre tracing the history of the Protestant Reformation across Europe — Luther, Zwingli, Calvin, Knox, and beyond — through manuscripts, early printed Bibles, paintings, and artefacts of the reformers and the churches they shaped.

name: Gutenberg Museum, Mainz
description: A museum in Johannes Gutenberg's home city dedicated to the history of printing, holding two original copies of Gutenberg's 42-line Bible (c. 1454-55) — the first substantial book printed in the West using movable type.
interest_note: A secular museum of printing history, not a Christian institution, but its Gutenberg Bibles are foundational artefacts in the story of how the Bible came to be mass-produced and widely read.
status: Main Liebfrauenplatz building closed for rebuilding as of 2026; interim exhibition 'Gutenberg Museum MOVED' at the Natural History Museum Mainz, Reichklarastraße 1

name: Musée du Désert, Mialet
description: The largest museum of French Protestant history, in the Cévennes hamlet where Camisard leader Pierre Laporte ("Rolland") was born. It traces the 1685-1787 "Desert" period, when Huguenots worshipped in hiding after the revocation of the Edict of Nantes, through some 3,000 objects, weapons, and documents of their resistance and endurance.

name: Museo Valdese, Torre Pellice
description: The historical museum of the Waldensians — a Christian movement tracing its origins to Peter Waldo in 1174, predating and later aligning with the Reformation, and severely persecuted for centuries in the Piedmont valleys. The museum traces their history alongside a reconstruction of traditional Waldensian valley life.

name: Melanchthonhaus, Wittenberg
description: The Wittenberg home of Philip Melanchthon, Martin Luther's close collaborator and the Reformation's principal systematic theologian, author of the Augsburg Confession. A UNESCO World Heritage building, its permanent exhibition traces his life, work, and lasting influence on Protestant theology and education.

name: Husitské muzeum (Hussite Museum), Tábor
description: Housed in Tábor's Old Town Hall, this museum traces the history of the Hussite movement — the reform movement that grew out of Jan Hus's preaching and martyrdom, and whose radical wing founded Tábor itself as a fortified town in 1420. Exhibits cover Hussite theology, the Hussite Wars, and daily life among the Taborites. Broader than a single-person memorial, it covers the movement Hus's death set in motion rather than Hus's own life, which is told at his memorials in Prague and Konstanz.

name: John Rylands Research Institute and Library, Manchester
description: The University of Manchester's Victorian neo-Gothic research library. In 2025 it became the new home of Dr Williams's Library — a dissenting Protestant library founded in London in 1729 — whose collection of some 135,000 volumes and manuscripts, including the papers of Puritan pastor Richard Baxter, now makes this the world's foremost single resource for the history of English Protestant Dissent and Nonconformity.
interest_note: A secular university library, but its historic Gothic reading room is free to visit, and the Dr Williams's Library collection it now holds is an unmatched resource on English Puritan, Presbyterian, Congregationalist, and Baptist history, open to researchers and the public through the Special Collections reading room.
status: Building open to the public, free admission; Special Collections reading room access by registration

name: Christian Brethren Archive, John Rylands Library, Manchester
address: John Rylands Library, 150 Deansgate, Manchester M3 3EH, England
description: The world's largest archive of manuscripts, books, and periodicals relating to the Christian (Plymouth) Brethren movement, established in 1979 at the University of Manchester Library. Its roughly 17,000 books, pamphlets, and tracts, plus assembly and individual records, include the personal papers of biblical scholar F. F. Bruce, who taught at Manchester as Rylands Professor of Biblical Criticism and Exegesis and encouraged the archive's founding.
status: Housed within the John Rylands Library; archive access to researchers by appointment through Special Collections
